---
title: "FW 536 — Day 4 afternoon lab (v3 · marine & stock assessment) — Your Name"
author: "Your Name Here"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    self_contained: true
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: show
    theme: cosmo
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

# Day 4 afternoon lab — Bayesian modeling problem set 1

**Problem set version:** **v3 — marine & stock assessment theme**.
(The companion version is [v2 — avian / songbird](problem_set_v2.html); you choose whichever theme fits your interests.)

> **Note on topic scope.** These problems use **Bayesian methods** covered in the Day 4 afternoon lecture (priors, posteriors, conjugacy, MCMC, Nimble). The morning problems (MLE-only) are in `morning_lab_template_v3.Rmd`.

This template matches [`problem_set_v3.html`](problem_set_v3.html). Write your work below, **Knit** to HTML, and upload the `.html` to the matching Canvas assignment.

For setup help see the [R Markdown tutorial](../rmarkdown_tutorial.html).

---

# Setup

```{r}
# Load packages you'll need. Add or remove as appropriate.
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(nimble)
library(MCMCvis)
library(coda)
```

---

# Problem 4 — Bayesian green sea turtle hatchling survival

> From a single nesting beach you released 200 marked green sea turtle hatchlings; underwater video and re-encounter surveys give a (rough) re-encounter count of 12 after 30 days. Prior literature on hatchling survival is sparse; treat it as $\phi \sim$ Beta(2, 18) (mean 0.10).

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 5 — Bayesian Schaefer biomass-dynamic model for a Pacific snapper

> You have $T = 15$ years of catch ($C_t$) and a CPUE index ($I_t$). The state-space Schaefer model is:

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 6 — Bayesian length-weight regression for Pacific cod

> The classical length-weight relationship is $W = a L^b$, fit on the log scale: $\log W_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\log a + b \log L_i, \sigma^2)$. Simulate 100 cod with $a = 0.01$, $b = 3.05$, $L \in U(20, 100)$ cm, $\sigma = 0.10$.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 7 — Bayesian zero-inflated negative binomial CPUE for blue shark

> A bycatch dataset of blue sharks on longline trips contains $n = 200$ trips with very many zeros and a long tail of large catches. Fit a Bayesian zero-inflated NB in Nimble.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 8 — Prior sensitivity on intrinsic growth rate $r$ for a long-lived rockfish

> Yelloweye rockfish are long-lived and slow-growing; biologists suspect $r$ is small. You have catch-and-index data but it is essentially uninformative for $r$ on its own.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 9 — BCI vs HPDI for MSY

> Suppose your posterior of MSY from Problem 5 is right-skewed (long upper tail). You want to communicate one number plus an interval to a fisheries manager.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 10 — Identifiability of catchability $q$ and biomass $B$

> The CPUE equation $I_t = q B_t$ means only the product $qB_t$ is identified from a single index. If you double $q$ and halve $B_t$, the model fits the data equally well.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# (Optional) Embed a photo of hand-written work

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE, out.width="60%", fig.cap="Hand-derivation for Problem N"}
knitr::include_graphics("images/problemN_derivation.jpg")
```

---

# Submission checklist

- [ ] Answered every afternoon problem from `problem_set_v3.html`.
- [ ] Knitted to HTML without errors.
- [ ] Photos (if any) embedded with `self_contained: true` in the YAML.
- [ ] Uploaded the `.html` to the matching Canvas assignment.
